title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At the Long Grass Hotel, John Samoys and Morin discuss household issues, including a dog eating chicken feed and a shortage of spinach. Nearby, Colonel Samson and Morley discuss Poirot, noting his French identity and suggesting they collect a seven-guinea installment from him.

After Polly helps Mrs. Samoyed pick up scattered beans, Mrs. Samoyed explains that the house is in disrepair because her husband's parents were poor and lacked funds for repairs after returning from India. She notes that they survive by taking in paying guests, including an elderly woman upstairs who has been bedridden and is scheduled to leave for her niece's home the following day. When Poirot pays the first week's rent of seven pounds and seven shillings, he identifies himself as a famous detective investigating the murder of Miss MacKnight. Mrs. Samoyed is dismissive, claiming the case is closed because the renter was convicted. After Mrs. Samoyed cuts her finger and blood drips onto the beans, Poirot declines to stay for lunch.

In a separate setting, Poirot interviews Mrs. Burch regarding the murder of her aunt. Mrs. Burch expresses annoyance at the police investigation and insists that James Bentley, the man convicted of the crime, is guilty. Poirot observes that Mrs. Burch is a tidy, capable woman and notes that Inspector Spence found no financial motive for the Burchs. Through questioning, Poirot learns that while the Burchs were blood relatives of the aunt, they were not close, though they knew she possessed a small fortune. He also learns that the aunt was a tidy woman who earned a daily wage doing odd jobs for the Carpenter family.
